title: The British Empire and its legacy
subject: history
courseDescription: The rise of the British Empire, the transatlantic slave trade and its abolition, imperial rule in India, decolonisation and legacy.
lesson:
The British Empire and its legacy

Key facts
Level: GCSE
Theme: British History
Period: c.1600 — 1997

Key question. How did Britain build the largest empire in history — and how should its legacy be judged?

At its height the British Empire ruled about a quarter of the world. It grew rich through trade — including the brutal transatlantic slave trade, later abolished (1807, 1833). India was its 'jewel'. After 1945, decolonisation ended the empire, leaving a debated legacy.

1 · A worldwide empire

By 1900 Britain ruled colonies on every continent — 'the empire on which the sun never sets'. It gained wealth and power through global trade, a strong navy and the resources of its colonies.

Term · The triangular trade
A three-way route: manufactured goods to Africa, enslaved people to the Americas, and plantation crops (like sugar) back to Britain. It made huge profits from slavery.

2 · Slavery and its abolition

Britain was central to the transatlantic slave trade. Millions of Africans endured the horrific Middle Passage to work on plantations. After long campaigns led by William Wilberforce, Britain abolished the slave trade in 1807 and slavery in 1833.

3 · India, the 'jewel in the crown'

India was the empire's richest colony, ruled first by the East India Company, then directly by the Crown from 1858. In the 20th century, Mahatma Gandhi led a non-violent independence movement. India became independent in 1947.

4 · Decolonisation and legacy

After 1945, colony after colony gained freedom — decolonisation. Many joined the Commonwealth. The empire's legacy is debated: it spread English and some institutions, but also brought slavery, conquest and exploitation.

In summary

Timeline

DateEvent
1600s-1700sEmpire and slave trade grow
1807Slave trade abolished
1833Slavery abolished in the empire
1858Crown takes direct rule of India
1947Indian independence
after 1945Decolonisation

Key terms

TermMeaning
Triangular tradeBritain-Africa-Americas slave route
Middle PassageBrutal Atlantic crossing of enslaved people
AbolitionEnding the slave trade / slavery
DecolonisationColonies becoming independent
CommonwealthFree association of former colonies

People

NameRole
William WilberforceLed abolition campaign in Parliament
East India CompanyRuled much of India for Britain
Mahatma GandhiLed India's non-violent independence movement
Watch out
The empire did not benefit everyone it ruled. It brought great wealth and power to Britain, but slavery, conquest and exploitation to many colonised peoples. Its legacy is genuinely debated — not simply 'good' or 'bad'.

The British Empire shaped the modern world's borders, languages and populations. Understanding both its power and its injustices is essential to understanding the world we live in today.

cards.recto: At its height, why was the British Empire called 'the empire on which the sun never sets'?
cards.verso: It was so large and worldwide that it was always daytime somewhere in it
cards.explanation: By 1900 Britain ruled a quarter of the world's land and people across every continent, so the sun was always shining on part of the empire.

medium: It was smaller than France's empire
hard: It was purely a trading company

cards.recto: Around 1900, roughly how much of the world's land did the British Empire control?
cards.explanation: At its peak the empire covered around a quarter of the globe and ruled hundreds of millions of people — the largest empire in history.

cards.recto: What was the forced transport of enslaved Africans across the Atlantic to the Americas called?
cards.explanation: Millions of Africans were kidnapped and shipped in brutal conditions to work on plantations. Britain was heavily involved and profited greatly.

cards.recto: The three-way route linking Britain, Africa and the Americas in the slave trade was called the...?
cards.explanation: Ships carried goods to Africa, enslaved people to the Americas, and plantation crops (like sugar) back to Britain — a hugely profitable triangle.

cards.recto: What was the brutal Atlantic crossing endured by enslaved Africans called?
cards.explanation: Enslaved people were packed into ships in horrific conditions. Many died on the Middle Passage from disease, cruelty and despair.

cards.recto: In which year did Britain abolish the slave trade across its empire?
cards.explanation: The Slave Trade Act of 1807 banned the trade in enslaved people. Slavery itself in most colonies was abolished later, in 1833.

cards.recto: Which MP led the long campaign in Parliament to abolish the slave trade?
cards.explanation: Wilberforce campaigned for decades alongside formerly enslaved people and abolitionists, helping to end the British slave trade in 1807.

cards.recto: In which year was slavery itself (not just the trade) abolished in most of the British Empire?
cards.explanation: The Slavery Abolition Act of 1833 freed enslaved people across most of the empire, though 'apprenticeship' delayed full freedom for some.

cards.recto: Which country was the most important colony, often called the 'jewel in the crown'?
cards.explanation: India was Britain's richest and most prized colony. It was ruled first by the East India Company, then directly by the Crown after 1858.

cards.recto: Which trading company first controlled large parts of India for Britain?
cards.explanation: This private company grew from trade to ruling millions in India, with its own army, until the Crown took over direct rule in 1858.

cards.recto: Who led a famous non-violent campaign for Indian independence from Britain?
cards.explanation: Gandhi used non-violent protest and civil disobedience to challenge British rule. India became independent in 1947.

cards.recto: In which year did India gain independence from Britain?
cards.explanation: India (and the new state of Pakistan) became independent in 1947. This helped begin the wider end of the empire.

cards.recto: What was the process of colonies becoming independent nations after 1945 called?
cards.explanation: After the Second World War, colony after colony gained independence. By the 1960s the British Empire had largely dissolved.

cards.recto: What voluntary association of Britain and its former colonies exists today?
cards.explanation: Many former colonies chose to remain linked in the Commonwealth, a free association of independent states — a legacy of empire.

cards.recto: [Compare] How is the empire's legacy remembered differently by different people?
cards.verso: Some stress trade, language and institutions; others stress slavery, conquest and exploitation
cards.explanation: The empire's legacy is debated. It spread the English language and some institutions, but also involved slavery, violence and the exploitation of colonised peoples.

easy: Everyone agrees it was entirely good
medium: It is only remembered as a sports league
It is remembered only in France
It had no effects to remember
hard: Only economists study it; no one debates it
It is seen the same way by everyone
It left no institutions or languages

cards.recto: [Watch out] Did the British Empire benefit everyone it ruled?
cards.verso: No; it brought profit and power to Britain but also slavery, conquest and exploitation for many colonised peoples
cards.explanation: A common error is to see empire as purely positive 'progress'. For many colonised people it meant loss of freedom, land and lives, alongside any trade or infrastructure.

medium: Yes, it ended all poverty in the colonies
Yes, it was purely a charity
No, because it never ruled anyone
hard: Yes, colonised peoples had full equal rights
No, because it only affected Britain

cards.recto: Overall, why does the British Empire still matter today?
cards.verso: It shaped the modern world's borders, languages, trade and populations, and its legacy is still debated
cards.explanation: The empire's impact endures in national borders, the spread of English, global migration and trade links — and in ongoing debates about its costs and benefits.

easy: It has no effect on the modern world
medium: It only concerned the Roman period
hard: It changed nothing about borders or language
It had no link to migration
